content:
# Genesis Chapter 39 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 22v–23r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_039_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_039_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 37–38 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0041

---

**1** And Joseph was brought down to Egypt, and Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, the captain of the guard, an Egyptian man, bought him from the hand of the Ishmaelites who had brought him down there.

**2** And the LORD was with Joseph, and he became a prosperous man, and he was in the house of his master the Egyptian.

**3** And his master saw that the LORD was with him, and that the LORD was making everything he did to prosper in his hand.

**4** And Joseph found favor in his sight, and he served him, and he appointed him over his household, and everything that was his he put into his hand.

**5** And it came to pass from the time that he appointed him over his household and over everything that was his, that the LORD blessed the house of the Egyptian on account of Joseph, and the blessing of the LORD was upon everything that was his, in the house and in the field.

**6** And he left everything that was his in Joseph's hand, and he paid no attention to anything he had except the bread that he ate. And Joseph was beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance.

**7** And it came to pass after these things that the wife of his master lifted up her eyes toward Joseph and said, "Lie with me."

**8** But he refused and said to the wife of his master, "Look, my master pays no attention to what is in the house, and everything that is his he has put into my hand.

**9** He is not greater in this house than I, and he has withheld nothing from me except you, because you are his wife. And how could I do this great evil and sin against God?"

**10** And it came to pass, as she spoke to Joseph day after day, that he did not listen to her, to lie beside her, to be with her.

**11** And it came to pass on a day like this that he came into the house to do his work, and there was none of the men of the household there in the house.

**12** And she seized him by his garment, saying, "Lie with me!" But he left his garment in her hand and fled and went outside.

**13** And it came to pass, when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand and fled outside,

**14** that she called to the men of her household and said to them, saying, "See, he has brought to us a Hebrew man to mock us. He came to me to lie with me, and I cried out with a loud voice.

**15** And it came to pass, when he heard that I raised my voice and cried out, that he left his garment beside me and fled and went outside."

**16** And she kept his garment beside her until his master came to his house.

**17** And she spoke to him according to these words, saying, "The Hebrew servant, whom you brought to us, came to me to mock me.

**18** And it came to pass, as I raised my voice and cried out, that he left his garment beside me and fled outside."

**19** And it came to pass, when his master heard the words of his wife that she spoke to him, saying, "According to these things your servant did to me," that his anger burned.

**20** And the master of Joseph took him and put him into the house of confinement — the place where the king's prisoners were confined — and he was there in the house of confinement.

**21** But the LORD was with Joseph and extended covenant-faithfulness to him, and gave him favor in the sight of the keeper of the house of confinement.

**22** And the keeper of the house of confinement put into Joseph's hand all the prisoners who were in the house of confinement, and everything that was done there — he was the one doing it.

**23** The keeper of the house of confinement paid no attention to anything that was in his hand, because the LORD was with him, and whatever he did the LORD was making to prosper.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources.

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 39 spans Folios 22v–23r across two manuscript images.

   **Folio 22v** (GEN_039_page_001.jpg): This is PDF page 43 of the West Semitic Research Project sequence — the same folio that carries the conclusion of Genesis 38 in its upper portion. The image is scanned in an inverted/rotated orientation (bottom-to-top), but the Hebrew text is fully legible when reoriented. The boundary between Genesis 38 and Genesis 39 is marked by a parashah (פ or ס) section marker following the birth of Zerah (38:30). The three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks is clearly legible throughout. The opening words of Genesis 39:1 — וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה (ve-Yosef hurad Mitsraymah — "and Joseph was brought down to Egypt") — are clearly visible at the start of a new section. The key terms יְהֹוָה (YHWH) occurring multiple times in verses 2–3 and 5 are legible with their characteristic full Tiberian pointing. The name פּוֹטִיפַר (Potiphar) in verse 1 is legible. The repetition of נָתַן בְּיָדוֹ ("put into his hand") in verses 4 and 6 is clearly visible as a formal refrain. The crucial term יָפֶה תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה (yefeh to'ar vi-yefeh mar'eh — "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance") in verse 6 is fully legible with its familiar doublet structure. The wife's command שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי (shikh'vah immi — "lie with me") in verse 7 is visible. The chapter appears to continue through approximately verse 15 on this folio.

   **Folio 23r** (GEN_039_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 23. This is PDF page 44. This page is photographed right-side up, with folio number 23 clearly visible at the top. The page carries the continuation of Genesis 39 from approximately verse 16 through verse 23, followed by the beginning of Genesis 40. The garment accusation scene (vv. 16–18), Potiphar's anger (v. 19), the imprisonment (v. 20), and the conclusion — יְהֹוָה הָיָה עִמּוֹ ("the LORD was with him," vv. 21, 23) — are all clearly visible. The key phrase בֵּית הַסֹּהַר (beit ha-sohar — "house of confinement") occurs repeatedly in verses 20–23 and is legible. The concluding phrase מַצְלִיחַ יְהֹוָה ("the LORD was making to prosper") in verse 23, echoing the same phrase in verses 2–3, is clearly visible. The parashah marker that signals the transition to Genesis 40 is visible at the bottom of the Genesis 39 section.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two Vaticanus pages were read directly.

   **Page 37** (GEN_039_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 37 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0041). The chapter header notation "CAP XLIIII" (= Genesis 39, in the Vaticanus chapter enumeration) is visible in the right margin of this page, marking the beginning of the Greek chapter. This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 38 in its upper portion, then transitions to Genesis 39, carrying the Greek text through approximately verse 18 of chapter 39. The continuous uncial script in three columns is fully legible. The opening phrase of Genesis 39 in Greek — Ιωσηφ δε κατήχθη εις Αίγυπτον ("and Joseph was brought down into Egypt") — is identifiable at the section's beginning. The key phrase ἦν κύριος μετὰ Ιωσηφ ("the LORD was with Joseph") in verses 2–3 is visible. The term ἐπίτροπος ("overseer/administrator") used for Joseph's promoted role is legible. The phrase εὔμορφος τῷ εἴδει καὶ ὡραῖος τῇ ὄψει ("beautiful in form and comely in appearance") in verse 6 — the LXX rendering of yefeh to'ar vi-yefeh mar'eh — is visible in the uncial text. The wife's demand and Joseph's refusal through approximately verse 18 is carried on this page.

   **Page 38** (GEN_039_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 38 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0042). This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 39 (vv. 19–23) and the beginning of Genesis 40. The chapter marker "CAP XLV" is visible in the right margin (Vaticanus chapter enumeration for Genesis 40), and a further notation at the bottom right reads "CAP XX," likely a parallel enumeration. The Greek text of the imprisonment narrative (vv. 19–23) is legible, including the key phrase ἦν κύριος μετὰ Ιωσηφ ("the LORD was with Joseph," v. 21), the term ὁ ἀρχιδεσμοφύλαξ (the "chief keeper of the prison") for the LC's שַׂר בֵּית הַסֹּהַר (sar beit ha-sohar — "commander of the prison house"), and the repetition of the LORD's presence and prosperity formula through verse 23.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Was brought down" — וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד (ve-Yosef hurad) (v. 1)**

The chapter opens with the waw-conjunctive וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה (ve-Yosef hurad Mitsraymah — "and Joseph was brought down to Egypt"). The verb form הוּרַד (hurad) is the Hophal (causative passive) third masculine singular of יָרַד (yarad — "to go down, to descend"): "he was caused to be brought down," i.e., "he was brought down." The Hophal passive is not common in narrative, and its use here is deliberate and theologically charged.

The descent motif in Genesis 37–39 is one of the most carefully controlled verbal patterns in the Joseph narrative:
- 37:25: The Ishmaelite caravan is "going down" (yordim) to Egypt.
- 37:35: Jacob threatens to "go down" (ered) to Sheol in mourning.
- 38:1: Judah "went down" (vayyered) from his brothers.
- 39:1: Joseph "was brought down" (hurad) to Egypt — passive, because he did not choose to go.

The shift from active descent (Judah: vayyered — "he went down," his own volition) to passive descent (Joseph: hurad — "he was brought down," against his will) is the text's formal marking of the moral distinction between the two stories juxtaposed in chapters 38–39. Judah descended by his own choice; Joseph was brought down by others. Judah's descent is morally ambiguous; Joseph's descent is an act of injustice done to him. The passive Hophal also preserves the divine perspective that will unfold through the chapter: Joseph is brought down, but the LORD is with him.

The formal translation renders "was brought down" — the literal Hophal passive — rather than the common smoothing "was taken down" or "had been brought down." The temporal relationship to Genesis 37:36 (where Potiphar already bought Joseph: וּפוֹטִיפַר שַׂר פַּרְעֹה קְנָה אֹתוֹ — "and Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, bought him") is deliberately recapitulated in 39:1 as the narrator resumes the Joseph thread after the Judah interlude. The LXX renders Ιωσηφ δε κατήχθη εις Αίγυπτον ("and Joseph was led/brought down into Egypt"), using the same passive construction (κατήχθη — aorist passive of κατάγω, "to bring down").

**2. "The LORD was with Joseph" — וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת יוֹסֵף (vayyehi YHWH et Yosef) (vv. 2, 3, 21, 23)**

The chapter's governing theological claim appears four times, forming an envelope structure that brackets both the first half (prosperity in Potiphar's house, vv. 2–3) and the second half (prosperity in prison, vv. 21–23) of the narrative:

- v. 2: וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת יוֹסֵף ("and the LORD was with Joseph")
- v. 3: כִּי יְהוָה אִתּוֹ ("that the LORD was with him")
- v. 21: וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת יוֹסֵף ("but the LORD was with Joseph")
- v. 23: כִּי יְהוָה אִתּוֹ ("because the LORD was with him")

This four-fold repetition is not narrative decoration; it is the chapter's theological spine. The formula "the LORD was with X" is a specific divine-presence formula in the Hebrew Bible — used of Isaac (26:28), of Ishmael (21:22 by analogy), and, in the form "the LORD is with you," addressed to Joshua (Deuteronomy 31:8) and Gideon (Judges 6:12). Its appearance in the Joseph narrative is both personal and programmatic: whatever happens to Joseph externally — elevation or imprisonment — the LORD's presence with him is constant and determinative.

The formula also functions structurally to bind the two halves of the chapter together. The chapter is often read as a story of reversal — Joseph goes from overseer to prisoner — but the divine-presence formula insists that there is no reversal from the LORD's perspective. The LORD is as fully present in the prison as in the palace. This is the chapter's most important theological claim, and the formal translation preserves it by rendering the formula identically each time it appears: "the LORD was with Joseph" / "the LORD was with him."

The waw-adversative in verse 21 (וַיְהִי — the same waw that begins v. 2) functions as a contrastive connector: "but the LORD was with Joseph." After Potiphar's anger (v. 19), after the imprisonment (v. 20), the narrative counters with this unchanged reality: "but the LORD was with Joseph." The formal translation renders the adversative with "but" to preserve the counter-movement the text performs.

**3. "Beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" — יְפֵה תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה (yefeh to'ar vi-yefeh mar'eh) (v. 6)**

The physical description of Joseph at the end of verse 6 uses the identical phrase applied to his mother Rachel in Genesis 29:17: וְרָחֵל הָיְתָה יְפַת תֹּאַר וִיפַת מַרְאֶה ("and Rachel was beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance"). The doublet יְפֵה תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה (yefeh to'ar vi-yefeh mar'eh) — literally "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" — uses two words for physical beauty:

- תֹּאַר (to'ar) — "form, outline, shape": the overall body shape and proportion.
- מַרְאֶה (mar'eh) — "appearance, sight, look": the visual impression, what is seen when one looks.

The phrase is thus a comprehensive statement of physical beauty — both in structure and in impression, both in form and in face. The narrator deploys this phrase only twice in Genesis: for Rachel (29:17) and for Joseph (39:6). The echo is unmistakable: Joseph has inherited his mother's beauty. This is not coincidental characterization; it is a narrative link between the two most beloved of Jacob's family — the wife he was deceived about and the son he nearly lost, both possessing the same exceptional beauty, both ultimately serving as the instruments of providential narrative.

The beauty of Joseph is immediately followed by the notice of the wife's desire (v. 7) — the physical description functions as a narrative setup for the temptation. The beauty that Joseph inherited from Rachel becomes the occasion for the danger that nearly destroys him.

The formal translation renders "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" — a word-for-word equivalent that preserves the doublet structure and carries the Rachel echo for readers attentive to the earlier usage. Modern translations that render "handsome in form and appearance" (ESV, NRSV) or "well-built and handsome" (NIV) lose some of the formal parallelism, though the meaning is equivalent. The formal translation's literal rendering preserves the deliberately identical phrasing of 29:17 and 39:6.

**4. "Sin against God" — חָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים (chatati l'Elohim) (v. 9)**

Joseph's refusal of Potiphar's wife culminates in the question: וְאֵיךְ אֶעֱשֶׂה הָרָעָה הַגְּדֹלָה הַזֹּאת וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים ("and how could I do this great evil and sin against God?"). The phrase חָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים (chatati l'Elohim — "I would sin against God") is significant on several levels.

The verb חָטָא (chata' — "to sin, to miss the mark, to do wrong") with the preposition לְ (le — "against, toward") constructs the act as a relational offense against a person — here, against God (Elohim). The form חָטָאתִי (chatati) is Qal perfect first person singular: "I would have sinned" or "I will have sinned" — a perfective used to describe a consequence of the action he is contemplating.

The use of Elohim rather than YHWH is theologically deliberate. Joseph is speaking to an Egyptian woman in an Egyptian context. He uses the more universal divine name — Elohim (God) — rather than YHWH (the covenant name of Israel's God). Yet his moral frame is entirely grounded in the character and claims of YHWH, who is with him (v. 2). The narrator has been using YHWH throughout the chapter (vv. 2–5); Joseph, in direct speech to a non-Israelite, uses Elohim — the name comprehensible across cultures. The moral gravity is the same: he names sexual betrayal of his master not merely as social or legal wrong but as offense against God himself.

This is the first explicit statement in the Joseph narrative of sin as primarily an offense against God rather than against a human party. Joseph has already noted the offense against his master — "he has withheld nothing from me except you, because you are his wife" (v. 9a) — the social and covenantal wrong. But he adds a second ground: "and how could I sin against God?" The ethical wrong is rooted in the theological wrong. This will resonate with Psalm 51:4 (David's confession: "against you alone I have sinned"), where the fullest articulation of this principle is made explicit.

The formal translation renders "sin against God" precisely, preserving Elohim as "God" (the appropriate rendering of the common divine name in contrast to the covenant name YHWH/the LORD).

**5. "The garment" — הַבֶּגֶד (ha-beged) (vv. 12–16)**

The word בֶּגֶד (beged — "garment, clothing") appears six times in Genesis 39 (vv. 12 [×2], 13, 15, 16, 18) as the central prop of the false accusation. This density of repetition is deliberate: the garment is not merely a physical item of clothing but the instrument of false testimony, the material evidence of a crime that did not occur.

The parallel with Genesis 37 is unavoidable. In chapter 37, Joseph's כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים (ketonet passim — "the ornamented/long-sleeved robe") was taken by his brothers, dipped in goat's blood, and presented to Jacob as evidence of his death: הַכֶּר נָא הֲכְתֹנֶת בִּנְךָ הִוא ("recognize, please, whether it is your son's robe or not" — 37:32). The garment was used as false evidence of Joseph's death. Now, in chapter 39, another garment — simply beged ("garment," a more ordinary word) — is seized by Potiphar's wife and used as false evidence of Joseph's assault.

The structural parallel is precise: in both cases, (1) Joseph's garment is left in someone's hands; (2) the garment is presented as physical evidence; (3) the evidence is used to destroy Joseph's position. In chapter 37 the garment stripped him of his identity in his father's eyes (making him "dead"). In chapter 39 the garment stripped him of his position in Potiphar's house (making him a criminal). Two garments, two false accusations, two descents — the verb יָרַד (yarad) applying to each.

The formal translation renders beged consistently as "garment" throughout — not "clothes" or "cloak" or "robe" — to preserve both the repetitive texture of the Hebrew and the echo of the robe motif from chapter 37.

**6. "Hebrew man" — אִישׁ עִבְרִי (ish ivri) (vv. 14, 17)**

Potiphar's wife, in her accusation to the household servants (v. 14) and then to her husband (v. 17), identifies Joseph with the ethnic designation אִישׁ עִבְרִי (ish ivri — "a Hebrew man"). The term עִבְרִי (ivri — "Hebrew") appears earlier in Genesis as an ethnic identifier: 14:13 calls Abraham הָעִבְרִי (ha-ivri — "the Hebrew"); the term occurs again in 40:15 (Joseph calls himself from "the land of the Hebrews") and in 41:12 (Joseph described as "a Hebrew youth"). In Exodus the term becomes prominent as the collective designation of Israel in Egypt.

The etymology of עִבְרִי (ivri) is disputed. The traditional connection to עֵבֶר (Ever), the ancestor listed in Genesis 10:24–25 and 11:14–17, makes it a patronymic — "descendants of Ever." An alternative hypothesis connects it to the Akkadian/Egyptian term Habiru/Apiru, a socioeconomic category denoting displaced or migrant people of uncertain status in the ancient Near East. If the Habiru connection is correct, עִבְרִי carries a social connotation alongside its ethnic one: Joseph is marked as a foreigner-laborer, a person of low social status, a "stateless" person. This makes the wife's use of the term in her accusation particularly pointed: she emphasizes his otherness as part of the accusation's framing — "he has brought us a Hebrew man to mock us" (v. 14). The foreigner is the threat; the servant is the abuser; the victim casts herself as the defender of domestic order against the interloper.

The formal translation renders "Hebrew man" (ish ivri) precisely, using the ethnic designation that the narrative itself uses. "Israelite" would be anachronistic (the name Israel is personal to Jacob and not yet extended to the people); "Hebrew" is the correct term here both textually and historically.

**7. "House of confinement" — בֵּית הַסֹּהַר (beit ha-sohar) (vv. 20–23)**

The term בֵּית הַסֹּהַר (beit ha-sohar — "house of confinement") is the narrative's consistent designation for the prison. The word סֹהַר (sohar) is unusual — it appears only in Genesis 39–40 in the Hebrew Bible, and its precise meaning has been debated. It derives from a root that may be related to סָחַר (sachar — "to go around, to surround") or may be a loanword. The Aramaic cognate sohar and the Arabic cognate suggest a round enclosure or enclosed space — hence "house of the round, enclosed place" — i.e., a jail, a prison, a place of confinement.

The LXX renders it consistently as οἰκία / δεσμωτήριον — the "house of bonds/chains" — confirming the carceral meaning. The formal translation renders "house of confinement" rather than "prison" or "jail" — "confinement" preserves the spatial and restrictive sense of the Hebrew without importing modern carceral associations, and "house" preserves the idiom of the Hebrew construct chain (beit X — "house of X").

The phrase occurs five times in verses 20–23, and the formal translation maintains "house of confinement" throughout — a literal rendering that preserves the repetitive, confining quality that the text itself enacts through repetition.

**8. "Extended covenant-faithfulness" — וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חֶסֶד (vayyeit elav chesed) (v. 21)**

Verse 21 describes the LORD's action toward Joseph in prison with three coordinated phrases: וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת יוֹסֵף וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חֶסֶד וַיִּתֵּן חִנּוֹ בְּעֵינֵי שַׂר בֵּית הַסֹּהַר ("but the LORD was with Joseph and extended covenant-faithfulness to him, and gave him favor in the sight of the keeper of the house of confinement"). The verb נָטָה (natah — "to stretch out, to extend, to incline") with the object חֶסֶד (chesed) is a specific collocation: "to extend/stretch out chesed" — meaning to actively reach out with covenant-faithfulness, to deliberately give it. The phrase occurs in Ruth 2:20 (Boaz has "not abandoned his chesed to the living and the dead") and Ruth 3:10 ("you have made this chesed greater than the first").

The word chesed is one of the Hebrew Bible's richest theological terms — conventionally translated "steadfast love," "lovingkindness," "mercy," "loyalty," "covenant love" — but none of these individually captures its full semantic range. Chesed is the quality of committed faithfulness that flows from a relationship of covenant or obligation — whether between God and Israel, between king and subject, between friends, or between God and individuals. It is not merely emotional affection but active, committed, reliable action in accordance with the relational bond. The LOGOS project renders it "covenant-faithfulness" (following the established convention noted in the manifest) — preserving both its relational and its active dimensions.

In verse 21, the LORD extends covenant-faithfulness to Joseph in prison — the same active divine commitment that has been present with him in Potiphar's house is now present with him in his cell. The instrument of that chesed is both direct (the LORD's own presence) and mediated (the favor of the prison keeper). The LORD works through the prison keeper's disposition, but it is the LORD who is the source. The formal translation renders "extended covenant-faithfulness to him" — preserving both the verb of active outreach (vayyeit — "he extended") and the technical term chesed.

**9. "To mock us" — לְצַחֶק בָּנוּ (letsachek banu) (v. 14)**

Potiphar's wife's accusation uses the verb צָחַק (tsachaq — "to laugh, to play, to mock, to sport"). The Piel form לְצַחֶק (letsachek) carries a range of meanings: "to mock, to make sport of, to abuse sexually." The construction לְצַחֶק בָּנוּ (letsachek banu — "to mock us / make sport of us") in verse 14 and then against me (בִּי — bi) in the version in verse 17 is the wife's framing of what she claims Joseph intended.

The word צָחַק is the same verbal root used for Isaac's name (יִצְחָק — Yitschak, "he laughs"), for the laughter of Sarah (18:12–15), and famously in Genesis 26:8 where Abimelech sees Isaac "sporting/playing" with Rebecca (מְצַחֵק — metsachek) — which in that context clearly implies sexual play. The wife uses a word that, in its Piel form especially, carries an undertone of sexual impropriety — "to sport with us / to abuse us." This makes her accusation self-servingly ambiguous: tsachek can mean "to mock" (humiliate by presence) or "to sport with" (sexual abuse). She frames Joseph's refusal as the crime.

The formal translation renders "to mock us" — capturing the primary accusation (Potiphar's wife claiming to be the victim of an assault/humiliation) without importing the secondary overtone, which would over-specify the ambiguity the Hebrew exploits.

**10. "His anger burned" — וַיִּחַר אַפּוֹ (vayyichar appo) (v. 19)**

The description of Potiphar's response to his wife's accusation — וַיִּחַר אַף אַדֹנֵי יוֹסֵף ("and the anger of Joseph's master burned") — uses the standard Hebrew idiom for intense anger: חָרָה אַף (charah af — literally "the nose/anger burned/was hot"). The same idiom describes God's anger in Exodus 4:14 and 32:10; Jacob's anger with Rachel in Genesis 30:2; and many other contexts. The "burning nose/anger" idiom may originate in the physical observation of a flushed, flaring nose as a sign of rage.

The verse notably calls Potiphar "the master of Joseph" (אֲדֹנֵי יוֹסֵף — adonei Yosef) rather than using his name — a subtle distancing that frames the action in terms of the master-servant relationship just being violated. The narrative does not editorialize about whether Potiphar believed his wife's accusation; it simply records his anger and his action. Scholars have noted that Potiphar's punishment of Joseph — imprisonment in the royal prison rather than execution — may hint at ambivalence: he imprisons Joseph rather than killing him, which would have been within his rights if the accusation were believed without reservation. The narrative does not comment; the formal translation reproduces the narrative's reticence.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 39

Pages 37–38 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible throughout both pages, with the characteristic Vaticanus hand visible. Page 37 (pa_0041) carries the conclusion of Genesis 38 and the Greek text of Genesis 39:1 through approximately verse 18. The chapter notation "CAP XLIIII" is visible in the right margin of page 37, marking the beginning of Genesis 39 in Vaticanus's chapter enumeration. Page 38 (pa_0042) carries the conclusion of Genesis 39 (vv. 19–23) and the beginning of Genesis 40, with "CAP XLV" visible in the right margin for the Genesis 40 chapter notation. Key variants and equivalences observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 39:1 | וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה ("and Joseph was brought down to Egypt") | Ιωσηφ δε κατήχθη εις Αίγυπτον ("and Joseph was brought down into Egypt") | MT followed | LXX consistent; κατάγω (to lead/bring down) correctly renders the Hophal hurad; passive preserved in both |
| 39:1 | שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים ("captain of the guard / chief of the butchers/executioners") | ἀρχιμάγειρος ("chief cook/chef") | "captain of the guard" (MT) | LXX renders טַבָּחִים (tabachim — "executioners, slaughterers, butchers") with μάγειρος ("cook/meat-carver") — the word's culinary sense rather than its military/executioner sense; MT's military meaning of "guard" commander is preserved in formal translation |
| 39:2 | וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת יוֹסֵף ("and the LORD was with Joseph") | ἦν κύριος μετὰ Ιωσηφ ("the LORD was with Joseph") | MT followed | LXX consistent; κύριος ("Lord") is LXX's standard rendering of YHWH |
| 39:2 | וַיְהִי אִישׁ מַצְלִיחַ ("and he became a prosperous man") | καὶ ἦν ἀνὴρ κατευθύνων ("and he was a man prospering/succeeding") | "he became a prosperous man" | LXX renders מַצְלִיחַ (matsliach — "prospering, causing to succeed") with κατευθύνω ("to direct successfully, to lead straight"); similar in meaning; MT's participial form suggests ongoing prosperity |
| 39:6 | יְפֵה תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה ("beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance") | εὔμορφος τῷ εἴδει καὶ ὡραῖος τῇ ὄψει ("beautiful in form and comely in appearance") | "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" (MT literal) | LXX uses two different words — εὔμορφος (beautiful/well-formed) and ὡραῖος (comely, in season/bloom, attractive) — whereas MT repeats the same root יָפֶה (yafeh) twice; MT's deliberate repetition of the same word is formally significant and preserved in translation |
| 39:9 | חָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים ("sin against God") | ἁμαρτήσομαι ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("I will sin before/in the sight of God") | "sin against God" | LXX uses future tense (ἁμαρτήσομαι — "I will sin") where MT uses a perfect used for consequence (chatati — "I would have sinned"); both convey prospective consequence; LXX adds ἐναντίον ("before, in the sight of") rather than the simple dative of agency; MT followed |
| 39:14 | לְצַחֶק בָּנוּ ("to mock us") | ἐμπαίζειν ἡμῖν ("to mock/play tricks on us") | "to mock us" | LXX ἐμπαίζω (to mock, to ridicule, to make sport of) is a consistent rendering of the Piel of tsachak |
| 39:14 | אִישׁ עִבְרִי ("a Hebrew man") | ἄνθρωπον Εβραῖον ("a Hebrew man") | "a Hebrew man" | LXX consistent; standard transliteration Εβραῖος for עִבְרִי |
| 39:20 | בֵּית הַסֹּהַר ("house of confinement") | δεσμωτήριον ("prison, house of bonds") | "house of confinement" (MT literal) | LXX uses the standard Greek term for prison δεσμωτήριον (from δεσμός — "chains, bonds"); MT's beit ha-sohar is a more specific architectural designation; formal translation preserves the Hebrew compound |
| 39:21 | וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חֶסֶד ("extended covenant-faithfulness to him") | καὶ ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ χάριν ("and gave him favor/grace") | "extended covenant-faithfulness to him" (MT) | LXX renders the full phrase vayyet elav chesed with simply "gave him favor" (χάριν — charin), collapsing the specific term chesed (covenant-faithfulness) into the more general χάρις (grace/favor); the MT's distinct verb of extension (natah) and its rich covenantal noun (chesed) are both preserved in formal translation |
| 39:22 | שַׂר בֵּית הַסֹּהַר ("commander of the house of confinement") | ὁ ἀρχιδεσμοφύλαξ ("the chief keeper of the prison") | "keeper of the house of confinement" | LXX uses a compound title ἀρχι-δεσμο-φύλαξ (chief-prison-keeper); MT's sar ("commander, captain, chief") is a more standard title; both designate the same figure; formal translation follows MT |
| 39:23 | לֹא הָיָה רֹאֶה אֶת כָּל מְאוּמָה ("paid no attention to anything") | οὐκ ἐπίστατο κατ᾽ αὐτόν οὐθέν ("he knew nothing concerning him / was in charge of nothing concerning him") | "paid no attention to anything that was in his hand" | LXX's rendering is ambiguous — ἐπίστατο can mean "he was in charge of" or "he knew of"; MT's "paid no attention" (literally "was not seeing anything") is clearer; MT followed |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Chiastic Architecture of Chapter 39**

Genesis 39 is constructed as a precise two-panel narrative, each panel governed by the same divine-presence formula and the same outcome of prosperity:

**Panel A — Potiphar's House (vv. 1–18):**
- vv. 1–6a: The LORD with Joseph → Joseph prospers → Joseph made overseer → complete trust
- v. 6b: Joseph's beauty described (narrative bridge)
- vv. 7–10: The wife's demand and Joseph's sustained refusal
- vv. 11–15: The garment seized — the crisis
- vv. 16–18: The false accusation delivered — the reversal

**Panel B — The Prison (vv. 19–23):**
- vv. 19–20: Potiphar's anger — Joseph imprisoned
- vv. 21–23: The LORD with Joseph → Joseph prospers → Joseph made overseer of all prisoners → complete trust

The structural parallelism between the two panels is meticulous. In Panel A, Potiphar "put everything into his hand" (v. 4: וְכָל אֲשֶׁר יֶשׁ לוֹ נָתַן בְּיָדוֹ — "everything that was his he put into his hand") and "paid no attention to anything he had" (v. 6: לֹא יָדַע אִתּוֹ מְאוּמָה — "he did not know anything with him"). In Panel B, the prison keeper "put into Joseph's hand all the prisoners" (v. 22: וַיִּתֵּן שַׂר בֵּית הַסֹּהַר בְּיַד יוֹסֵף אֵת כָּל הָאֲסִירִים) and "paid no attention to anything that was in his hand" (v. 23: לֹא הָיָה רֹאֶה אֶת כָּל מְאוּמָה בְּיָדוֹ). The language is nearly identical, deliberately so. Joseph is an overseer — first of a household, then of a prison — in both cases because the LORD is with him. The externals change; the divine presence and its effect do not.

This double structure reflects a central theological principle of the Joseph narrative: prosperity (matsliach — "success") is not defined by circumstances but by divine presence. Joseph in prison is as prosperous as Joseph in Potiphar's house, because the LORD is as present in the prison as in the palace. The chapter does not resolve the injustice done to Joseph — it does not exonerate him in public, does not restore him to freedom — but it insists, through the structural repetition of the presence formula, that no external reversal can alter the fundamental reality of divine accompaniment.

**The Descent That Does Not Prevail**

The descent motif (yarad / hurad) that runs through chapters 37–39 reaches its lowest point — literally — in chapter 39. Joseph has been:
- Thrown into the pit (bor) by his brothers (37:24)
- Sold to Ishmaelites (37:28)
- Sold to Potiphar (37:36; 39:1) — "brought down" (hurad)
- Accused falsely by Potiphar's wife (39:14–18)
- Imprisoned in the royal prison (39:20)

Each stage is a further descent — social, geographical, and legal. The Hophal passive hurad ("was brought down") with which the chapter opens is the linguistic signal of this trajectory. Yet the chapter also begins the countermovement. The divine-presence formula (the LORD was with Joseph) appears first precisely at the lowest point — in the household of an Egyptian official, as a slave — and appears again at the still lower point of imprisonment. The descent does not prevail against the LORD's presence; the text insists on this not by resolving the descent but by asserting the presence despite and within it.

This theological structure anticipates the full resolution of chapters 41–45: the same Joseph who was "brought down" to Egypt will be "lifted up" by Pharaoh to the second position in Egypt. But the text resists a simple reversal narrative by placing the divine-presence formula at every point of the descent — signaling that the LORD's faithfulness does not wait for the ascent before it becomes operative.

**The Two False Garments: A Narrative Diptych**

Genesis 39's beged (garment) forms a diptych with the ketonet passim (ornamented robe) of Genesis 37. In each case:

1. A garment belonging to Joseph is taken from him.
2. The garment is presented as evidence of a crime against the person who seized it.
3. The person who receives the evidence is deceived by it.
4. Joseph's fortunes are catastrophically reversed.

In Genesis 37: The brothers take the robe → dip it in blood → present it to Jacob → "recognize, please" → Jacob believes Joseph is dead.
In Genesis 39: The wife seizes the garment → presents it to the servants → presents it to Potiphar → "according to these words" → Potiphar believes Joseph is guilty.

The verbal echo is not the hakker-na of chapter 38 but the structural repetition of garment-as-evidence. In both cases, the false evidence is materially Joseph's own possession; in both cases, the deception is complete and devastating; in both cases, Joseph is silenced — the text gives him no defense in either scene.

What the text does differently in the two episodes is the location of the truth. In chapter 37, only the reader and the brothers know the truth — Jacob never learns it. In chapter 39, again only the reader knows the truth — Potiphar acts on false evidence and presumably never knows otherwise. Joseph's vindication in both cases is not social (he is not publicly cleared) but divine (the LORD is with him). The resolution of both false accusations will come only in chapters 45–50, when Joseph himself names the providential meaning of all his descents: "you intended evil against me, but God intended it for good" (50:20).

**"How Could I Sin Against God?" — Moral Theology in a Foreign Household**

Joseph's refusal of Potiphar's wife in verses 8–9 is one of the most theologically rich speeches of the entire Joseph narrative. His argument proceeds in stages:

1. **Horizontal loyalty** (v. 8–9a): He identifies the betrayal of trust as the first ground of refusal — his master has given him everything and trusted him completely. Sexual relations with the master's wife would be a violation of this specific trust relationship. This is an argument from covenant-loyalty.

2. **Categorical exclusion** (v. 9b): "He has withheld nothing from me except you, because you are his wife." The exception is not arbitrary; it is structured by the marriage covenant — she is specifically excluded because of her covenantal status as his master's wife. Joseph names the category precisely.

3. **Vertical accountability** (v. 9c): "How could I do this great evil and sin against God?" Having argued from horizontal loyalty (to his master) and categorical exclusion (her covenantal status), Joseph grounds his final refusal in vertical accountability. The action would be not only a betrayal of a human relationship but an offense against God.

The phrase "this great evil" (הָרָעָה הַגְּדֹלָה הַזֹּאת — ha-ra'ah ha-gedolah ha-zot) is striking. Joseph does not minimize the moral gravity of the act by calling it an indiscretion or a mistake; he names it "great evil" (ra'ah gedolah). The same term ra'ah appears in 37:20 (the brothers plan "evil" against Joseph) and 50:20 (Joseph says "you planned evil against me"). Joseph, who has been the object of ra'ah, refuses to become its agent — naming it with the same gravity his brothers' act deserved.

This speech functions as the chapter's theological center. Joseph is not merely an obedient servant following social codes; he is a man who operates from a coherent moral theology — loyalty, covenant categories, and divine accountability — that holds even under sustained daily pressure (v. 10) and in the absence of any human witness to his fidelity. The chapter frames his imprisonment as the direct consequence of this moral fidelity: Joseph went to prison not despite his righteousness but because of it, at the hands of a woman whom his righteousness frustrated. This is one of the Joseph narrative's most searching theological claims.
